topic: help writing a follow-up email to a client

turns.content: can you help me write a follow-up email to a client? I've drafted something but it reads really awkward and I've rewritten it 4 times already
Of course — send me what you've got and I'll help tighten it up. What's the context? What are you following up on?

so we had a meeting last week where we presented some preliminary findings from our analysis. the client said they'd review internally and come back with questions but it's been a week and nothing. my manager wants me to nudge them without being pushy
Got it — the classic "gentle nudge that doesn't sound like a nudge." What's the tone of your relationship with this client? Formal and corporate, or have you built a bit of rapport?

somewhere in between. like it's professional but the main contact is pretty approachable. I've been on a few calls with him. here's what I have so far:

'Dear James, I hope you're well. I wanted to follow up on our meeting last Tuesday where we shared the preliminary findings of our analysis. I wanted to check if you had any questions or feedback, and whether there's anything further you'd like us to look into. Please let me know if you'd like to arrange a call to discuss. Kind regards, Aisha'

That's actually not bad — it's clear and polite. The main issue is it reads a bit generic and passive. A few tweaks to make it feel more purposeful:

"Hi James,

Hope you're well. I wanted to follow up on our session last Tuesday — specifically the findings around [mention one specific thing you presented, e.g. the working capital position or the revenue adjustments]. If anything flagged questions on your side, happy to jump on a quick call to walk through it in more detail.

We're also progressing the next phase of the analysis, so if there are any particular areas you'd like us to prioritise, it would be helpful to know sooner rather than later.

Let me know what works.

Best,
Aisha"

The key changes: referencing something specific from the meeting shows you're paying attention, and the line about prioritising the next phase gives them a practical reason to respond rather than just "do you have questions."

oh that's bare better than mine. the bit about prioritising the next phase is smart because it gives them a reason to reply even if they don't have questions. can I change 'session' to 'meeting' though? we don't really use session
Absolutely — use whatever language is natural for your team. "Meeting" or "discussion" both work. The wording should match how you'd normally talk to this client. Any other adjustments?
yeah actually — should I cc my manager on this? she told me to send the nudge but I don't know if cc'ing her makes it feel more formal or like we're putting pressure on him

Good instinct to think about that. If you cc your manager, it does subtly raise the stakes — the client sees a more senior name and might feel more compelled to respond, which could be exactly what your manager wants. But if the relationship is mostly between you and James, adding her could change the dynamic. I'd suggest: send it from you without the cc, and if there's still no response in 3-4 days, then your manager can follow up directly. That way you've tried the soft approach first. But check with your manager — she might specifically want to be visible on it.
yeah I'll ask her. she's pretty reasonable about this stuff. last question — what time should I send it? like is there a best time for these kinds of emails or am I overthinking it

You're slightly overthinking it but it's not a bad thing to consider. Tuesday through Thursday morning is generally the sweet spot for getting email responses — Monday inboxes are flooded and Friday people are mentally checked out. Mid-morning around 9:30-10am is ideal because it lands after they've cleared their overnight emails but before the midday meeting rush. If you're sending today, just send it — any time is better than waiting another day.

it's Monday today so I'll schedule it for tomorrow morning then. thanks for this honestly, I always overthink client emails. like I spend 30 minutes on a 5 sentence email and then still feel like it's wrong

That's really common, especially when you're conscious of how every email reflects on you and the firm. But honestly, your original draft was fine — you just needed to make it slightly more specific and give the client a reason to act. The more of these you send, the faster you'll get at finding the right tone. You clearly already have good instincts about professional communication.
appreciate that tbh. right I'm scheduling it now and then I'm going to stop staring at it. thanks for the help
Schedule it and walk away — don't re-read it a fifth time. It's good. Hope James comes back quickly.
